source_text: Smoking causes tar buildup in the lungs, which in turn causes lung cancer.

distant_compatible.title: Smoking -> Lung Cancer (Direct Cause, Tar as Implication)

auxiliary_assumptions.description: Tar buildup is a causal mediator vs. a conceptual byproduct
auxiliary_assumptions.reason: The phrase 'which in turn causes' could mean tar buildup is in the causal chain (mediator) or merely a co-occurring implication of smoking that is itself conceptually linked to cancer.
auxiliary_assumptions.closest_match_choice: Tar buildup as causal mediator: C1 ->c-> C2 ->c-> C3
auxiliary_assumptions.distant_compatible_choice: Direct causation C1 ->c-> C3, with tar buildup as conceptual implication of both smoking and cancer
other_options: Tar as a predictive (p) indicator rather than causal mediator

auxiliary_assumptions.reason: The text implies a temporal sequence (smoking -> tar -> cancer) but does not explicitly rule out feedback effects.
auxiliary_assumptions.distant_compatible_choice: Unidirectional (same choice - text is clear on direction)

underspecification_points.impact: Cannot determine the magnitude of any causal effect. Both models leave strength as null.
underspecification_points.text_location: Entire sentence - no quantification language used.

underspecification_points.impact: Unclear whether this applies to all smokers, heavy smokers, specific populations, etc.
underspecification_points.text_location: No scope language present in the text.

underspecification_points.impact: The text does not mention whether other factors also contribute to tar buildup or lung cancer.
underspecification_points.text_location: Entire sentence - no hedging or qualification language.

translation_notes.model_divergence_summary: The primary model treats tar buildup as a causal mediator in a chain (Smoking -> Tar -> Cancer). The alternative model treats smoking as a direct cause of lung cancer, with tar buildup reinterpreted as a conceptual implication rather than a causal step. Both readings are compatible with 'which in turn causes', which is ambiguous between temporal mediation and loose sequential narration.

rigor_assessment.description: Assessment based on T-items from Musfeld et al. Indicators for the Assessment of Theoretical Rigor (v3.0).

T1_phenomenon.justification: The phenomenon (lung cancer occurrence) is implied but not explicitly described as the target of explanation.

T2_concept_definitions.justification: None of the three concepts are formally defined. Their meanings are assumed from common usage.

T3_mechanism.justification: A causal chain is asserted but the mechanism by which each step operates is not described.

T4_scope.justification: No conditions, populations, or boundary conditions are mentioned.

rigor_assessment.overall_assessment: This sentence is a minimal causal claim with no concept definitions, no mechanism detail, and no scope conditions. The underspecification is reflected in the model divergence: even the basic causal structure (mediation vs. direct effect) cannot be determined from the text alone.
